title: Containers created with 5.4.2 fail to start with 5.5.0
body:
### Issue Description

Arch Linux testing repositories recently incorporated podman 5.5.0 which I have installed for testing. The runtime I used is runc 1.3.0 as shipped by Arch Linux.

I have a few containers created with podman 5.4.2 which were working fine before this upgrade. When I try to `podman start` these containers they fail with the following message: `Error: unable to start container "<container id>": runc: runc create failed: unable to start container process: error during container init: open /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ping_group_range: no such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found`.

I initially reported the issue to Arch Linux packaging [here](https://gitlab.archlinux.org/archlinux/packaging/packages/podman/-/issues/10). Their very helpful follow-up seems to indicate this is not a packaging issue. They have also provided the results of a git bisection which identifies where between 5.4.2 and 5.5.0 this issue started cropping up.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Try to create and run a new container along the following lines with podman 5.5.0 and runc 1.3.0:

```
podman run \                                        
  --gpus all \
  --interactive \
  --tty \
  --dns none \
  --userns=keep-id \
  --group-add keep-groups \
  --mount type=volume,source="test_root",destination=/ \
  --mount type=volume,source="test_user",destination=/home/user \
  --name "test" \
  --hostname "test" \
  docker.io/library/archlinux:latest
```

The aforementioned error message appears and the container is created but does not start. The host also has nvidida-container-toolkit 1.17.7 installed and the correct CDI generated. The exact same configuration with only podman version downgraded to 5.4.2 starts fine.

Removing either ` --gpus all` or ` --mount type=volume,source="test_root",destination=/` allows the container to start with podman 5.5.0.

### Describe the results you received

Above error message and container did not start.

### Describe the results you expected

That container would start and be attached to as it did under podman 5.4.2.

### podman info output

```yaml

  ociRuntime:

    version: |-

  os: linux

    executable: /usr/bin/pasta

  remoteSocket:
```

### Podman in a container

No.

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless.

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes.

### Additional environment details

Nothing to add.

### Additional information

Using crun instead of runc causes a different error to appear under both 5.4.2 and 5.5.0 when the same podman command line is executed.

comments.body:
Note that `keep-groups` is not supported in runc. It only works with crun.

If a bisect points to https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/88b62d2c27211fddf920a8d9b7d136ba58bbf544 then it also likely means it is not a direct podman problem but rather something that got introduced by a dependency bump.

Note sure how this is supposed to work but `--mount type=volume,source="test_root",destination=/` overmounts the entire root with would hide /proc assuming /proc was mounted before so that explains why that would fail with ENOENT.
However I don't see why this would change between the two podman versions I wonder if the cdi code that was update in that commit changed the mount order in the container spec.
Can you provide the output of `jq .mounts $(podman inspect --format {{.OCIConfigPath}} <container_name> )` (replace with actual container name) for the working and non working version?

> Can you provide the output of `jq .mounts $(podman inspect --format {{.OCIConfigPath}} <container_name> )` (replace with actual container name) for the working and non working version?

> Note sure how this is supposed to work but `--mount type=volume,source="test_root",destination=/` overmounts the entire root with would hide /proc assuming /proc was mounted before so that explains why that would fail with ENOENT. However I don't see why this would change between the two podman versions I wonder if the cdi code that was update in that commit changed the mount order in the container spec.

The command line I use might be incorrect due to my ignorance of container technology details. My intention with that line is to "copy up" the image (archlinux:latest from Docker image library) root filesystem into a named volume. My understanding was that it would be best to have the volatile parts of a container defined as volumes and in this case I intended to regularly update the container using the package manager running inside it to have a development environment similar to but isolated from my host's Arch Linux installation. Making it a named volume would also make it easier for me to keep track of a number of such containers configured ad hoc for different kinds of development work.

What I can say for certain is that the aforementioned command line worked as I hoped it would as of podman 5.4.2 but stopped working with the upgrade to 5.5.0. With 5.5.0 I could neither create and run new containers of this sort, nor run the older containers of this sort that I already had made.

Yeah as I thought the `/` mount is order after `/proc` now. Not sure why this changed. Likely somthing with the cdi update shown in that bisect commit.

crun doesn't seem to be able to mount it `/` like that with runc it works and it seems like the normal copy up happened.

```
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: mount `/home/pholzing/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes/test/_data` to ``: Invalid argument
```
cc @giuseppe 

> The command line I use might be incorrect due to my ignorance of container technology details. My intention with that line is to "copy up" the image (archlinux:latest from Docker image library) root filesystem into a named volume. My understanding was that it would be best to have the volatile parts of a container defined as volumes and in this case I intended to regularly update the container using the package manager running inside it to have a development environment similar to but isolated from my host's Arch Linux installation. Making it a named volume would also make it easier for me to keep track of a number of such containers configured ad hoc for different kinds of development work.

I mean this is the kind of things we normally strongly discourage, a container should have a static image and you should recreate the container with a newly updated image every time.

Of course many people don't like that and fair enough if it works it works for them. To me this sounds like you want to look at projects like toolbx and distrobox.

 
But regardless if this used to work we should look into exactly what changed to make it work again as we like to avoid any regressions of course.

@giuseppe I don't think this is a crun issue. This is in particular about the regression when using runc and cdi with podman 5.5.0.

Yes crun cannot mount the root which is certainly a bug in itself but not related to this regression in podman so I think this should be moved back to podman, I am working on a podman fix at the moment.
sure I can move this back, I was looking at the crun issue right now and was preparing a patch.  I'll move it back and fill a new one for crun
@myrslint Any chance you can verify this with https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26162?

> [@myrslint](https://github.com/myrslint) Any chance you can verify this with [#26162](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26162)?

I applied #26162 on top of tag v5.5.0 and compiled using the existing Arch Linux PKGBUILD. I can confirm podman 5.5.0 with that patch now runs both my older 5.4.2 containers created using above command line and new ones created thus. Thank you!

I'm also grateful for the pointers to Distrobox and Toolbox. I may be able to replace my current setup with one of those.
